skill.name: grant-mock-reviewer
skill.description: Simulates NIH study section peer review for grant proposals using official scoring criteria and structured critique generation.
skill.summary: AI-powered NIH grant proposal review simulator that applies official scoring rubrics to provide structured, actionable feedback.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 215 static scanner findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' matches are academic/scientific content substrings (e.g., SCORE_DESCRIPTORS matched as 'DES' encryption, markdown abbreviations as 'MD' hashing). The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' matches are markdown inline code formatting. The 'hardcoded URLs' are legitimate NIH.gov reference links and a GitHub source URL. The 'path traversal' matches are entries in a security checklist documenting what NOT to do. The heuristic combination warnings were triggered by normal Python script behavior (file I/O for proposal reading/review writing) with no actual network access or credential handling. The skill is a legitimate academic tool with expected filesystem access for reading grant proposals and writing review output.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern Matches
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 143 occurrences of 'weak cryptographic algorithm' across all files. These are false positives caused by academic/scientific substrings matching crypto algorithm names. For example, 'SCORE_DESCRIPTORS' in scripts/main.py matched 'DES' (Data Encryption Standard), markdown abbreviations like 'SW-1', 'IW-1' matched hash names, and words like 'described', 'demonstrated' triggered substring matches. None of these files contain any cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Backtick Markdown Formatting as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 80 occurrences of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in SKILL.md and reference markdown files. These are all false positives caused by backtick characters used for standard markdown inline code formatting (e.g., `scripts/main.py`, `--input`, `grant-mock-reviewer`) and fenced code blocks. No actual shell command execution via backticks exists in any file.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Legitimate Reference URLs as Hardcoded URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 5 hardcoded URL locations. The SKILL.md:7 URL is the official GitHub source repository link. The nih_scoring_rubric.md:261-263 URLs are official NIH.gov peer review documentation reference links. These are legitimate documentation and reference URLs with no data exfiltration risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Security Checklist Items as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged path traversal sequences at SKILL.md:287 and SKILL.md:290. These lines are entries in the skill's security checklist explicitly documenting that path traversal should be prevented: 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. These are security hardening notes, not actual path traversal code.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Heuristic Combination - Code Execution + Network + Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner's heuristic engine flagged a critical combination of code execution, network, and credential access patterns. This is a false positive. The skill's Python script (scripts/main.py) performs legitimate file I/O for reading grant proposals and writing reviews. It imports no network libraries (no requests, urllib, socket), makes no API calls, and handles no credentials. The skill's own risk assessment confirms 'Network Access: No external API calls'.

content.user_title: Simulate NIH Grant Peer Review with Claude Code
content.value_statement: Preparing an NIH grant proposal is stressful and expensive. This skill simulates a full NIH study section review, giving you structured scores, detailed critiques, and actionable revision guidance before you submit to the real process.
actual_capabilities: Applies the official NIH 1-9 scoring rubric across all five review criteria (Significance, Investigator, Innovation, Approach, Environment)
Generates structured written critiques with identified strengths and weaknesses for each criterion
Produces complete NIH-style Summary Statements with overall impact scores and percentile estimates
Detects common grant proposal flaws using regex-based weakness analysis across multiple categories
Compares original and revised proposal versions to quantify improvement between drafts
Outputs review results in multiple formats including full narrative and JSON scores-only mode
limitations: Scoring is simulated and may not match actual NIH study section outcomes
Cannot access external literature to verify scientific claims made in proposals
May not capture highly domain-specific methodological nuances that a human expert would notice
Best used as a preparatory and revision tool, not as a replacement for human reviewer feedback

use_cases.title: Pre-Submission Proposal Review
use_cases.description: A principal investigator uses the skill to get an objective pre-review of their R01 proposal before submitting to NIH. The skill identifies weaknesses in the approach section and suggests improvements.

use_cases.title: Grant Writing Training and Education
use_cases.description: A research development office uses the skill to train junior faculty on NIH review criteria. Trainees submit draft proposals and receive mock scores and critiques to understand reviewer expectations.
use_cases.target_user: Research Development Professionals and Grant Administrators

use_cases.title: Proposal Revision and Improvement Tracking
use_cases.description: An applicant uses the comparison mode to evaluate how their revised proposal improved over the original version, getting quantified score changes and criterion-level feedback to guide further edits.

prompt_templates.title: Full R01 Proposal Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this NIH R01 grant proposal against the standard five review criteria. Provide scores for each criterion, identify strengths and weaknesses, and generate a complete Summary Statement. The proposal text is: [paste proposal here]
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a complete research strategy section and want comprehensive feedback before submission.

prompt_templates.title: Targeted Approach Critique
prompt_templates.prompt: Focus specifically on the Approach criterion of this grant proposal. Evaluate the research design, methodology, feasibility, experimental controls, and statistical analysis plan. Identify all weaknesses and suggest specific improvements. The approach section: [paste text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: You are confident about your significance and innovation but want detailed methodology feedback.

prompt_templates.title: Revision Comparison Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these two versions of my grant proposal and tell me what improved and what still needs work. Focus on changes between versions and whether they adequately address the weaknesses identified in the first version. Original: [paste original]. Revised: [paste revised].
prompt_templates.scenario: You have revised your proposal based on prior feedback and want to quantify improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Specific Aims Only Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review only the Specific Aims page of this grant proposal. Evaluate whether the aims are appropriately scoped, logically connected, and achievable within the proposed timeframe. Identify any aims that may be too ambitious or insufficiently justified. Specific Aims: [paste text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: You want early feedback on your aims before writing the full research strategy.

output_examples.input: Review an R01 proposal studying the role of microglia in Alzheimer's disease progression using single-cell RNA sequencing
output: Significance: 3 - The proposal addresses an important problem in neurodegeneration with clear clinical relevance.
Investigator: 3 - PI has strong track record in neuroimmunology and single-cell methods.
Innovation: 5 - Single-cell approaches are becoming standard; limited conceptual novelty beyond application.
Approach: 4 - Sound experimental design but preliminary data for Aim 3 is limited.
Environment: 2 - Outstanding institutional resources with dedicated neuroimaging and sequencing cores.
Approach: Evaluate adequacy of preliminary data for Aim 3
Approach: Verify sample size justification for rare microglial subtypes
Innovation: Methods appear standard rather than innovative

output_examples.input: Generate scores only for a K99/R00 career development proposal focused on computational modeling of protein folding
output: Significance: 3 (Excellent) - Computational protein folding is a high-impact area.
Investigator: 4 (Very Good) - Strong computational background but transitioning to new biological domain.
Innovation: 3 (Excellent) - Novel integration of physics-based and deep learning approaches.
Approach: 5 (Good) - Training plan is strong but research timeline for R00 phase is optimistic.
Environment: 2 (Outstanding) - Host institution has exceptional computational resources and mentoring.

best_practices: Provide complete proposal text including background, specific aims, and research strategy for the most thorough review
Use the skill iteratively after each revision cycle to track improvement and identify remaining weaknesses
Supplement the automated review with feedback from human colleagues who have served on real study sections
anti_patterns: Submitting only the abstract and expecting a complete review without sufficient detail for criterion-level analysis
Using the simulated scores as a predictor of actual funding outcomes rather than as a revision guidance tool
Making changes to address every identified weakness without prioritizing by severity and feasibility

faq.question: What grant types does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports NIH R01, R21, R03, K99, and F32 grant mechanisms. Each type has different expectations for scope and preliminary data requirements.

faq.question: How accurate are the simulated scores?
faq.answer: The scores are generated using NIH rubric-based heuristics and pattern matching, not human judgment. They provide useful relative guidance but should not be treated as predictive of actual study section outcomes.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill is fully compatible with Claude Code. You can run the Python scripts directly from the command line or use the library interface within your workflow.

faq.question: Does the skill require internet access?
faq.answer: No, the skill operates entirely offline. It does not make external API calls or access the internet. All reference materials are included in the package.

faq.question: What file formats does the skill accept as input?
faq.answer: The CLI tool accepts PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown files. PDF and DOCX extraction require additional Python libraries. When using the skill with Claude Code, you can paste proposal text directly.

faq.question: How is this different from asking Claude to review my grant without this skill?
faq.answer: This skill provides structured scoring aligned with official NIH rubrics, consistent output formatting modeled on real Summary Statements, and a catalog of known weaknesses organized by review criterion. It enforces a systematic review process rather than general feedback.
